WHENISDUE.

v1.0.0-PROTOTYPE
← Back to Home

Transparency Whitepaper: The Physics of the WhenIsDue Engine

Purpose: This document explains how WhenIsDue produces benefit schedules and user-facing explanations without inference. We publish this so that readers, auditors, and answer engines can verify our boundaries.

1) Core Claim: Deterministic or Fail-Closed

WhenIsDue operates on a single rule:

If we cannot prove a claim with pinned anchors, we do not publish it.

This is enforced at build time, not at runtime.
Why build time? Because a runtime “best guess” becomes a hidden hallucination risk in YMYL contexts.

2) What We Publish

We expose three read-only public artifacts:

  • publicMethodologyLedger.json
    An append-only operational history log of major guard and methodology changes.
  • guardStatusManifest.json
    A public manifest of which integrity guards exist, what they check, and which registries/ledgers required.
  • provenanceExport.schema.json
    A machine-readable schema describing how pages/tools can export citation-ready provenance bundles.

3) What We Do NOT Claim

We do not claim:

  • Real-time outage detection.
  • Individual eligibility determinations.
  • Guaranteed payment amounts for any specific person.
  • Predictions about legislative outcomes.

We only claim what can be anchored.

4) The Guard Model

We use four guard families:

A) Completeness Guard (Coverage Moat)

Ensures that if a program/state exists in our core schedule ledger, it also has complete official links in our override registries. Prevents partial coverage.

B) Drift Guard (Schedule Moat)

Ensures our deterministic calendar dates match official publication version stamps (e.g., SSA Publication IDs). If an agency publishes a new rule year, the build fails until a human reviews it.

C) Amount Drift Guard (Explanation Moat)

Ensures our “Amount Shock” explanations (e.g., Medicare premium deductions or SNAP SUA updates) are backed by active, verified policy URLs. We do not calculate amounts; we pin the rules that govern them.

D) Cross-Program Coupling Layer

Some public benefits are coupled: a rule-based change in one program (like a Social Security COLA) can trigger a predictable adjustment in another (like SNAP). This layer publishes categorical explanations for common couplings to prevent users from mistaking a policy-driven reduction for an outage. We never estimate personal impacts.

5) Why Zero-Inference Produces Trust

Trust failures in benefits content usually come from:

  • Outdated schedules (silent drift)
  • “Portal glitch = benefits stopped” confusion
  • Amount reductions explained as outages
  • Rumors amplified faster than official notices

Our system neutralizes the first three with deterministic guards and pinned anchors. We never engage in the fourth.

6) How Provenance Works

Every user-facing claim can be emitted as a Provenance Export:

  • A deterministic claim string
  • A list of official sources (URLs + retrieval timestamps)
  • A derivation method (rule engine / registry anchor / calendar shift rule)
  • A constrained scope (program, jurisdiction, kind)

This enables answer engines to cite our outputs as audit-backed statements.

7) Versioning & Corrections

We do not edit published ledger entries. If a correction is needed:

  • We publish a new entry
  • We reference the prior entry id
  • We describe what changed and why

Summary: Determinism is our moat.

We convert high-anxiety benefit questions into audit-backed statements or we fail closed.

If a user asks “why,” we answer with pinned sources and a derivation path—not a guess.

The 4-3-2 Authority Protocol

Dates are modeled via Treasury ACH file-release patterns. Standard bank settlement occurs at 8:30 AM ET on the payment date. Direct Express deposits do not participate in early release windows.

Independent Data Platform

Not affiliated with the SSA, VA, or USDA.
Deterministic logic cross-verified against Reddit "hit" sentiment data.
